coredata.dc:title: Designing of sustainable, solid-state and photoluminescence switchable electrospun nanofibrous PVA/WTR-CDs hybrid films: A photophysical study
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Gore, Anil H.
Patil, Akshay S.
Gunjal, Datta B.
Naik, Vaibhav M.
Waghmare, Ravindra D.
Patil, Chandrashekhar S.
Kolekar, Govind B.
coredata.dc:description: Herein we proposed a sustainable solid-state photoluminescent nanofibrous hybrid films fabricated through electrospining of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and carbon dots (CDs). The CDs employed during the fabrication hybrid films were derived from waste and abundantly available carbonaceous waste tea residue (WTR) precursor. More interestingly, the photoluminescent PVA/WTR-CDs hybrid films were able to switch the increased amount of incorporated WTR-CDs simply under UV light. The as prepared nanofibrous PVA/WTR-CDs hybrid films were further characterized by different techniques in order to confirm the successful and homogenous incorporation WTR-CDs into PVA matrix viz.UV–vis spectroscopy, Field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), Fluorescence spectroscopy and microscopy, Fourier transform infra red spectroscopy (FT-IR). The photophysical investigations of all the films (pristine and hybrid) with respect to its liquid solutions revealed that the intrinsic properties of liquid WTR-CDs were well restored in solid-state nanofibrous hybrid films of PVA/WTR-CDs. Furthermore, morphological analysis via FE-SEM showed uniform and smooth nanofibers in a PVA films with average diameter 0.104 μm and were increased with increased amount of WTR-CDs from 0.104 to 0.170 μm and 0.104 to 0.258 μm for PVA-WTR-CDs-1 and PVA-WTR-CDs-10 hybrid films respectively. All these characteristics of designed solid-state photoluminescent nanofibrous hybrid films opens new avenues in the field of optical sensing, optoelectronics, biological, healthcare, food and related multipurpose applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photophysical
Nanofibrous
Photoluminescent
Carbon dots
Waste tea residue
Hybrid films

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of nanofibrous photoluminescent hybrid films
Photophysical study of nanofibrous photoluminescent (PVA/WTR-CDs) hybrid films
Study under visible and UV light

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Overlaid fluorescence excitation and emission (λex = 315 nm) spectra and (b) HR-TEM image of WTR-CDs liquid solution.
FE-SEM images of nanofibrous (a) PVA film, (b) PVA/WTR-CDs-1 hybrid film and (c) PVA/WTR-CDs-10 hybrid film. Inset images showing flexibility characteristic of corresponding films.
Photographic images of nanofibrous PVA film, PVA/WTR-CDs-1 hybrid film and PVA/WTR-CDs-10 hybrid film under visible light (a) and its corresponding images under UV light (b).
UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) PVA, (b) WTR-CDs-1, (c) PVA/WTR-CDs-1, (d) WTR-CDs-10 (c) PVA/WTR-CDs-10 liquid solutions.
UV–vis DRS spectra of nanofibrous (a) PVA film, (b) PVA/WTR-CDs-1 hybrid film and (c) PVA/WTR-CDs-10 hybrid film.
Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) PVA, (b) WTR-CDs-1, (c) PVA/WTR-CDs-1, (d) WTR-CDs-10 (c) PVA/WTR-CDs-10 liquid solutions (λex = 315 nm).
Fluorescence emission spectra of nanofibrous (a) PVA film, (b) PVA/WTR-CDs-1 hybrid film and (c) PVA/WTR-CDs-10 hybrid film (λex = 315 nm).
FT-IR spectra of nanofibrous (a) PVA film, (b) PVA/WTR-CDs-1 hybrid film and (c) PVA/WTR-CDs-10 hybrid film.
Fluorescence microscopic images of nanofibrous (a) PVA film, (b) PVA/WTR-CDs-1 hybrid film and (c) PVA/WTR-CDs-10 hybrid film under bright field and its corresponding images at λex=350 nm (a'-c').

head.ce:title: Designing of sustainable, solid-state and photoluminescence switchable electrospun nanofibrous PVA/WTR-CDs hybrid films: A photophysical study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Rajarshi Chhatrapati Shahu College, Kolhapur, 416005, MS, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fluorescence Spectroscopy Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur, 416004, MS, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Rajarshi Chhatrapati Shahu College, Kolhapur, 416005, MS, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein we proposed a sustainable solid-state photoluminescent nanofibrous hybrid films fabricated through electrospining of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and carbon dots (CDs). The CDs employed during the fabrication hybrid films were derived from waste and abundantly available carbonaceous waste tea residue (WTR) precursor. More interestingly, the photoluminescent PVA/WTR-CDs hybrid films were able to switch the increased amount of incorporated WTR-CDs simply under UV light. The as prepared nanofibrous PVA/WTR-CDs hybrid films were further characterized by different techniques in order to confirm the successful and homogenous incorporation WTR-CDs into PVA matrix viz.UV–vis spectroscopy, Field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), Fluorescence spectroscopy and microscopy, Fourier transform infra red spectroscopy (FT-IR). The photophysical investigations of all the films (pristine and hybrid) with respect to its liquid solutions revealed that the intrinsic properties of liquid WTR-CDs were well restored in solid-state nanofibrous hybrid films of PVA/WTR-CDs. Furthermore, morphological analysis via FE-SEM showed uniform and smooth nanofibers in a PVA films with average diameter 0.104 μm and were increased with increased amount of WTR-CDs from 0.104 to 0.170 μm and 0.104 to 0.258 μm for PVA-WTR-CDs-1 and PVA-WTR-CDs-10 hybrid films respectively. All these characteristics of designed solid-state photoluminescent nanofibrous hybrid films opens new avenues in the field of optical sensing, optoelectronics, biological, healthcare, food and related multipurpose applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, carbon based fluorescent nanomaterials especially carbon dots (CDs) have been attracted terrific interest in the field of nanoscience and nanotechnology for their charming photophysical and photochemical properties [1–4]. As well, CDs also exhibits some distinctive properties viz., good biocompatibility, chemical inertness, low toxicity, aqueous photostability, and comparable quantum yield with excitation dependent fluorescence emission across the whole visible spectrum [5,6]. Moreover, large scale synthesis of CDs at laboratory level is quite possible due to accessibility of simple, economical, greener and diverse synthesis methods as well as easily, inexpensive, copious, and sustainable availability of required carbon sources [7,8]. Because of all these, CDs have been created a unique place in the field of sensing, detection, imaging, tissue engineering, drug delivery, energy, photonic and optoelectronic devices [9–18]. However, long-term photophysical study of CDs in colloidal state is quite difficult due to self-quenching and concentration quenching of photoluminescence which leads to limited use of it for practical applications in various fields.

In order to overcome this problem researchers have been started to incorporate CDs in polymeric matrix through various techniques such as film casting, spin-coating etc. [19–24]. Incorporation of CDs into a polymer matrix is an advantageous for enhancing mechanical strength and chemical stability of CDs necessary for real time applications. Moreover, it also helps to control the agglomeration of CDs by restoring original photophysical properties. However, solid-state hybrid films prepared via aforementioned methods cannot be used on large scale in practical applications because of increased thickness, non-uniform distribution of CDs, preparation requires large quantity of polymers, increased weight etc. Besides, CDs incorporated polymeric film have not been able to overcome the problem of self-absorption, concentration quenching, and decreased quantum yield (QY) in solid-state. Thus, construction, designing and development of solid-state CDs hybrid film which preserves intrinsic properties in a polymeric matrix becomes a great challenge.

According to the previous study, it appeared that, Li et al first time successfully incorporated aqueous fluorescent CdTe QDs in poly (vinyl alcohol) by the use of electrospining technique with restored photophysical properties in an nanofiber matrix which may offer possible way to design solid-state fluorescent nanofiber film [25]. Thereafter, more comparably fluorescent electrospun polyvinyl alcohol/CdSe@ZnS nanocomposite fibers have been also fabricated by Emre Atabey and co-workers [26]. Even though traditional semiconductor quantum dots (CdS, CdSe, ZnSe, CdTe etc.) and its nanocomposites (CdSe@ZnS, CdTe@ZnO, PbSe etc.) possess outstanding fluorescence properties, their core is based on heavy metals which limits their applicability in biological sciences [27]. By virtue of this, during last couple of years researchers have reported various papers describing methodology for manipulation of fluorescent nanofibrous mat containing carbon based nanomaterials for diverse applications. Thus, more related to carbon based fluorescent nanomaterials Hak-Yong Kim group has successfully prepared optically transparent and photoluminescent electrospun nanofiber composed of carbon quantum dots in a polyacrylonitrile and polyacrylic acid blend [28]. Chohye Lee et al., has prepared biocompatible, photoluminescent and multipurpose electrospun mat containing carbon quantum dot in keratin/poly (vinyl alcohol)/nanofiber [29]. Research group of Qunhui Yuan has also developed luminescent carbon quantum dot encapsulated mesoporous silica/polyacrylonitrile electrospun nanofibrous membrane and its sensing performance was checked for various metal ions [30]. Min Lin et al. presented an inner filter effect based sensor for the detection of tetracycline antibiotic with the aid of solid-state nanofibrous mat consisting of carbon nanodots loaded electrospun polyvinyl alcohol [31]. Researcher of Nieves Murillo and Virginia Ruiz group proposed that photostability and sensing performance electrospun polyacrylonitrile nanofibrous filtering membrane can be enhanced by encapsulating graphene quantum dots in to it [32]. More recently Yue Zhai et al constructed an efficient solid-state photoluminescence carbon dot/polyvinylpyrrolidone nanofibrous hybrid by utilizing electrospining technique [33].

Although aforementioned reports provide an idea about construction solid-state CDs photoluminescent electrospun nanofibers hybrid for multipurpose applications, their large scale production is somewhat difficult because required carbon dots are prepared from chemical precursors with complicated methodologies which ultimately increase the cost of production. On the contrary, some used polymers are not biocompatible and biodegradable so cannot be used in biological, healthcare, food and related applications. Consequently, we inspired, owing to disadvantages of existing hybrid films, to fabricate solid-state photoluminescent CDs polymeric electrospun hybrid consisting of sustainable, green, and abundant carbon dots derived from easily available sources by incorporating into biocompatible and biodegradable polymeric matrix through electrospinning.

Herein we have profitably incorporated sustainable and green CDs synthesized from kitchen derived waste tea residue (WTR) in a biodegradable and biocompatible polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as a polymeric matrix through electrospinning to get photoluminescent nanofibrous hybrid film. In addition to that, detailed photophysical study of as prepared photoluminescent nanofibrous hybrid film was also carried out with respect to its liquid solutions. Moreover, successful incorporation of WTR-CDs in PVA matrix was also proved by UV–vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, FE-SEM, Fluorescence microscopy, FT-IR measurements.

Polyvinyl alcohol (Mw = 85,000–124,000, 99+% hydrolyzed) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India.The waste tea residue powder was collected from different canteens in the campus on large scale. All the other common reagents and chemicals were of analytical grade and used as such without any further purification. Double distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

A various instrumental technique was used to characterise the liquid WTR-CDs, nanofibrous PVA and PVA/WTR-CDs hybrid films. The UV–vis absorption spectra of liquid solutions were recorded on double beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (Specord 210 plus, Analytik jena, Germany) whereas UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of all the nanofibrous solid films were recorded using UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV 3092, Lab India, India). The fluorescence (excitation and emission) measurements of all the liquid solutions and nanofibrous solid hybrid films were recorded on spectrofluorometer (FP-8300, Jasco, Japan) from 320 to 600 nm upon excitation at 315 nm with 5-5 nm excitation and emission slit width respectively. The functional groups of pristine PVA and PVA/WTR-CDs nanofibrous hybrid films were also characterised by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) Spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). High resolution-transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of WTR-CDs solution were obtained on Transmission electron microscope (FEI, Tecnai G2, F30, 300 kV, Japan) by placing a drop of liquid on the carbon-coated copper grid and dried at ambient temperature. The surface morphology of the PVA and PVA/WTR-CDs nanofibrous hybrid films were analysed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Mira-3, Tescan, Czech Republic). Fluorescent microscopic analysis of nanofibrous films were carried out on Zeiss Axio-scope A 1 (DAPI) 19 and observed under DAPI filter (Chroma3100) comprising 25 mm diameter filters, excitation filter (D350/50), beam splitter (400 DCLP) and emission filter (D460/50).

Luminescent carbon nanodots (CDs) essential to make fluorescent hybrid polymeric film was prepared as per our earlier report [34]. As a source carbonaceous material, kitchen derived waste tea residue (WTR) was utilized for the synthesis of CDs in aqueous media. A WTR is economical, sustainable and globally accessible source containing residues of sugar, milk etc. Briefly, as collected WTR powder was firstly dried at room temperature and crushed into fine powder. Then, the desired amount of WTR powder were carbonised (200 °C for 5 h) in a muffle furnace using silica crucible under controlled conditions. After that, requisite amount finely divided carbon powder was then dispersed into distilled water and stirred overnight. Furthermore, resultant aqueous mixture was filtered, centrifuged (12,000 rpm) and passed through syringe filter (0.22 μm) in order to obtain WTR-CDs. Finally, aqueous solution of WTR-CDs was diluted to specific volume and kept at the freeze temperature for further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of nanofibrous photoluminescent hybrid films
ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous polymer solution of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, 6 wt%) was prepared by dissolving calculated amount of PVA in distilled water with stirring at 90 °C till to get clear solution. Then electrospun solutions (10 mL) containing only PVA, PVA/WTR-CDs-1 and PVA/WTR-CDs-10 were prepared by adding 0, 1.0 and 10 mg of WTR-CDs into PVA solutions with stirring to get homogenous solutions. To fabricate nanofibrous PVA and photoluminescent PVA/WTR-CDs hybrid film; 10 mL of aforementioned polymer blend were transferred for electrospining under optimised conditions. An electrospining experiments were carried out at voltage of 18 kV by keeping 10 cm distance between the needle tip and rotating drum collector wrapped with aluminium foil. The flow rate of 0.5 mL/h was kept constant using plastic syringe of capacity 5.0 mL. At last, after completion of experiments nanofibrous electrospun films were peeled out from the aluminium foils and pieces were stored at room temperature in sample container. The prepared nanofibrous films were named as PVA film (pristine), PVA/WTR-CDs-1and PVA-WTR-CDs-10 hybrid film and used for further study.

WTR-CDs used for electrospining are primarily characterized for its emission and excitation properties in liquid state by fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 1 (a) shows excitation and emission spectra of WTR-CDs. It was observed that sharp and narrow shape of excitation maxima at about 315 nm while an emission maximum at 420 nm reveals uniform and narrow size distribution of WTR-CDs in aqueous solution. Moreover, to know the size and shape of the prepared WTR-CDs, HR-TEM characterisation was implemented. Fig. 1(b) depicts the HR-TEM image of WTR-CDs with homogenously distributed spherical shaped CDs. The average size of the WTR-CDs is within the range of 2–10 nm.

The FE-SEM is a tempted technique in material science for morphological investigation of different materials. Therefore, it was employed to examine the surface nature of nanofibrous PVA and PVA-WTR-CDs hybrid films. Fig. 2 (a–c) displays FE-SEM images of electrospun PVA film in absence and presences of 1 mg and 10 mg WTR-CDs respectively. Fig. 2(a) apparently shows exceptionally smooth, defect less surface and almost uniform sized distribution of electrospun nanofiber of PVA film suggested précised optimization for electro spinnability. The average diameter of the nanofibers in the PVA film is 0.104 μm. Although, PVA-WTR-CDs-1 and PVA-WTR-CDs-10 hybrid films have likely fine, smooth morphology to PVA film, the average diameter of nanofibers increased from 0.104 to 0.170 μm and 0.104 to 0.258 μm respectively. This increased diameter may be due to the added WTR-CDs into PVA films, confirms successful incorporation of WTR-CDs in nanofibrous PVA film. FE-SEM images are unable to identify loaded WTR-CDs, owing to their nanometer size, into the PVA by naked eye, therefore fluorescence microscopic analysis is the best technique to know the incorporation of WTR-CDs since it is highly fluorescent in nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical study of nanofibrous photoluminescent (PVA/WTR-CDs) hybrid films
ce:section.ce:para: As our goal is to incorporate the WTR-CDs into PVA film via electrospining and fabricate photoluminescent nanofibrous hybrid films therefore it is important to characterize these fabricated photoluminescent nanofibrous hybrid films. The morphological evaluation and photophysical study were carried through different techniques to confirm successful incorporation of WTR-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study under visible and UV light
ce:section.ce:para: Emission behavior of fluorescent material is recognized by exposing it to the UV light which is straightforward methodology and can be used in traditional laboratories. To judge the emission behavior of as prepared hybrid films we have carried out experiments under UV light, because absorption of WTR-CDs lies in UV region and subsequent emission in visible (blue) region. Fig. 3 (a) and (b) shows photographic images of nanofibrous PVA film, PVA/WTR-CDs-1 hybrid film and PVA/WTR-CDs-10 hybrid film under visible light and its corresponding images under UV light respectively. In Fig. 3(a), all the nanofibrous films are white in color under visible light and there are no any distinct changes were observed by naked eye with increasing concentration of WTR-CDs. However, nanofibrous PVA film was viewed under UV light shows blue color while PVA/WTR-CDs-1 and PVA/WTR-CDs-10 hybrid films showed strong blue-greenish colored emission and it was more pronounced with increasing concentration of WTR-CDs. These photographic images simply confirm the homogeneous distribution of WTR-CDs and successful fabrication of photoluminescent solid-state hybrid films.

To know the absorption profile of individual PVA, WTR-CDs, and its mixture with varying amount of WTR-CDs, we have carried out absorption measurement in liquid state which is shown in Fig. 4 . It was observed that the peak intensity around 275 nm of PVA/WTR-CDs mixture is higher than that of PVA and WTR-CDs solutions. This may be due to the contribution of absorbance from UV active WTR-CDs to PVA solution. To confirm this phenomenon, we have again increased the concentration of WTR-CDs from 1.0 mg to 10 mg and eventually found similar observation with great enhancement in absorbance of PVA hybrid films (Fig. 4). Similarly, in order to get insight of incorporation of WTR-CDs into PVA electrospun nanofibrous films, the UV–vis absorption study of these nanofibrous films was also carried out. Fig. 5 shows DRS absorption spectra of nanofibrous PVA film, PVA/WTR-CDs-1 and PVA/WTR-CDs-10 hybrid film. It demonstrates that all these samples do not have well absorption peak in the spectrum while it shows absorption in the range of 250–300 nm and their maximum absorption seems to be around the 275 nm. The absorption intensity of these hybrid films compared to PVA film found to be increased with the increasing concentrations of WTR-CDs. These results are in accordance with the observations from the study of system in liquid state and thus evidenced the successful incorporation of WTR-CDs into the PVA matrix.

The fluorescence spectra of PVA, WTR-CDs and PVA/WTR-CDs in liquid solution were measured and depicted in Fig. 6 . The emission spectrum of WTR-CDs is centered at 420 nm when excited at 315 nm while PVA do not have noticeable emission band in the region of 350–600 nm. Conversely, by adding 1.0 mg of WTR-CDs into the PVA solution, resultant mixture shows emission around 420 nm without any spectral shift in the wavelength and at same altitude with mere WTR-CDs-1 owing to the highly emissive nature of WTR-CDs and not from PVA. Similar observations are found with increased concentration of WTR-CDs for PVA/WTR-CDs-10 liquid. To impart photoluminescence to the nanofibrous PVA film more similarly we have added increasing amount of WTR-CDs through electrospining technique and their emission properties are investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 , the nanofibrous PVA film has no any emission maxima at around 420 nm while WTR-CDs (1.0 mg) embedded PVA film shows tiny shoulder like emission band around 420 nm. Furthermore, with addition of increased concentration of WTR-CDs (10 mg) into PVA polymer minute emission band of nanofibrous PVA/WTR-CDs-10 film around 420 nm, which accounts for WTR-CDs emission, becomes distinguishable. Thus, these photoluminescence studies corroborate homogeneous distribution of incorporated WTR-CDs in the PVA nanofibrous film.

The surface functionality of PVA, PVA/WTR-CDs-1 and PVA/WTR-CDs-10 films are characterized by FR-IR spectroscopy ranging from 4000−800 cm−1. Fig. 8 depicts FT-IR spectrum of (a) PVA, (b) PVA/WTR-CDs-1 and (c) PVA/WTR-CDs-10. In spectrum (a) the broad peak at 3373 cm-1 is ascribed for OH stretching vibrations. The peaks at 2908, 1706 and 1371 cm−1 which are attributed for the stretching vibrations of CH2, CO and CO bond respectively. In addition to this, terminal polyvinyl alcohol groups (CC) of PVA film is responsible for strong absorption at 1093 cm-1 while 1425 cm-1 band is assigned for CH2 deformation. After incorporation of WTR-CDs in PVA matrix, the absorption intensity of nanofibrous hybrid films (PVA/WTR-CDs-1 & PVA/WTR-CDs-10) is tremendously increases with the concentration of WTR-CDs throughout the region and this is due to the absorptive nature of incorporated WTR-CDs. The multiple peaks in PVA/WTR-CDs hybrid films at 3504, 3448 and 3351cm−1 is contribution of NH and OH vibrations from added WTR-CDs (spectrum b and c). Moreover, the more intense band of CH, CO, CH2 deformation, CO and terminal CC at 2898, 1707, 1433, 1242 and 1089 cm−1 respectively suggests the successful incorporation of WTR-CDs in the PVA films.

Fluorescence microscopy is widely used technique to know the loading of different fluorescent material on electrospun nanofiber from years. Therefore, we have carried out microscopic analysis study of as prepared nanofibrous films under bright field and by exciting samples at 350 nm. The photographic images of nanofibrous (a) PVA film, (b) PVA/WTR-CDs-1 hybrid film and (c) PVA/WTR-CDs-10 hybrid film under bright field and its corresponding images at λex=350 nm (a'-c') are presented in Fig. 9 . It was observed that for pristine PVA nanofibrous films, there is no any color emission seen under bright field as well as at 350 nm (a and a'). While on the other hand there is blue colored emission was observed in both PVA/WTR-CDs-1 (b and b') at 350 nm and becomes more intense in case of PVA/WTR-CDs-10 film (c and c') indicated retaining of the original fluorescence of WTR-CDs. The fluorescent nature of WTR-CDs was well visualized inside the both PVA/WTR-CDs hybrid films which clearly reflect the uniform distribution of incorporated WTR-CDs throughout the PVA nanofiber as well as enhanced emission intensity via increased concentration (b' to c'). These results suggest that intrinsic fluorescence behavior of WTR-CDs was well conserved and successfully incorporated inside the nanofibrous PVA films.

Briefly, we designed concentration dependent photoluminescence switchable solid-state PVA/WTR-CDs nanofibrous hybrid films via electrospining. Photoluminescent hybrid films were fabricated by utilizing commonly available, biodegradable, biocompatible polymer (PVA) and CDs derived from worldwide accessible waste based carbonaceous precursor such as waste tea residue (WTR). Hence, large scale production of films is quite possible with utilizing waste and protocol becomes economical, sustainable and suggesting new way for environmental remediation. All characterization proved successful and homogenous incorporation of WTR-CDs into PVA matrix with original fluorescence characteristic of liquid WTR-CDs. Thus, as designed PVA/WTR-CDs nanofibrous hybrid with this route can be employed for versatile applications in the area of optical sensing, optoelectronics, biological, healthcare, foods etc.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Author Dr. Anil H. Gore gratefully acknowledges DST-SERB, New Delhi, for providing research grants under young scientist scheme project (File no. YSS/2015/001806).
